channel.title: Jax Morning Brief
channel.description: Your daily rundown of national headlines, Jacksonville and Northeast Florida local news, home lending updates, and the latest in AI. Produced by Akilesh.
channel.itunes:author: Akilesh

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Warsh Path Clears, Iran Talks Collapse, Brent Above 105
item.description: Tillis drops his Fed-chair blockade after DOJ ends the Powell probe. Trump cancels his envoys' Pakistan trip as oil pushes past the level that could flip mortgage rates. Plus Google's 40-billion-dollar Anthropic bet.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning, and welcome back from the weekend. It's Monday, April 27th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: A weekend with two huge stories pulling in opposite directions. The path is suddenly clear for Kevin Warsh to take over the Federal Reserve, after Senator Thom Tillis dropped his blockade on Sunday.

ANDREW: And Iran peace talks went the other way. Trump canceled his envoys' trip to Pakistan on Saturday, Brent crude is now above 105 dollars a barrel, and that has direct consequences for the mortgage market we'll get into in a few minutes.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. Friday was a record-setting close. The S and P 500 gained 0.8 percent to finish at 7,165, an all-time high. The Nasdaq added 1.6 percent to close at 24,836, also a record, with semiconductors notching their 18th straight up day and Nvidia retaking the 5-trillion-dollar market cap mark. The Dow was the outlier, down a tenth of a percent to 49,230. The ten-year Treasury yield finished the week at 4.31 percent, and the 30-year fixed mortgage rate sits at 6.32 percent according to Mortgage News Daily. But futures are pointing lower this morning as Iran talks falter and oil pushes higher.

ANDREW: And while you were away from the news, three things moved. The Justice Department dropped its criminal probe of Fed Chair Jerome Powell on Friday, which set off the Warsh news over the weekend. Trump killed the Pakistan talks on Saturday. And Google announced it's putting up to 40 billion dollars into Anthropic. We'll take all three.

ANDREW: Let's start with the Fed, because the politics broke open over the weekend in a way nobody expected this fast.

ANDREW: On Friday afternoon, U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro announced the Justice Department is closing its criminal investigation into Jerome Powell. That probe had been the entire reason Senator Thom Tillis, the North Carolina Republican, was blocking Trump's nominee Kevin Warsh from advancing through the Senate Banking Committee. Tillis had said all along he wouldn't vote to advance Warsh while the DOJ was investigating the sitting Fed chair. The probe drops Friday. Tillis drops his block on Sunday. The Banking Committee now has a vote scheduled for Wednesday.

JENNY: So just to lay out the timeline, because this changes everything we were tracking last week. Powell's term ends May 15th. If the committee votes Wednesday and the full Senate moves quickly after that, can Warsh actually be confirmed before the deadline?

ANDREW: Yes. With Tillis on board, Republicans have the 13 to 11 committee margin to advance him, and the math on the floor was never the problem. The expectation now is that Warsh is confirmed in the first or second week of May, before Powell walks out the door. So the gap scenario we were planning for, where Vice Chair Philip Jefferson would have served as acting chair, is probably off the table.

JENNY: And what changed at DOJ? Last we heard a federal judge had quashed their subpoenas back in March.

ANDREW: That's right. Judge James Boasberg said in March there was no evidence Powell had committed any crime other than displeasing the president. Pirro's office referred the matter to the Fed's Inspector General and closed the criminal piece on Friday. That gave Tillis the off-ramp he needed. Whether the timing was coincidental or coordinated is the question reporters are chasing this morning.

ANDREW: One more thing on the Fed. Warsh in his confirmation hearing on April 21st told senators he wouldn't be Trump's, quote, sock puppet. That line is going to follow him into the job. The first real test is whether his Fed cuts in June, when markets are pricing roughly a 70 percent chance of a quarter-point move. Warsh has historically been more hawkish than Powell on inflation, so a Warsh-led Fed that cuts in June would be a meaningful signal that the economic data is doing the talking, not the White House.

JENNY: And the political read on this? Trump has spent months hammering Powell publicly. Warsh slides in saying he'll be independent. Does the market actually believe that?

ANDREW: The Treasury market's vote so far is yes. The ten-year actually ticked lower Friday after the DOJ news, which is the bond market saying it expects a credible Fed chair, not a rubber stamp. If that read changes, you'll see it first in the long end of the curve.

JENNY: That's a clean handoff to mortgages, because rates are the other side of that same Fed coin. Andrew, where are we?

ANDREW: We're in a strange spot. The headline rate is good. Mortgage News Daily has the 30-year at 6.32 percent as of Friday. The Freddie Mac weekly survey, which trails by a few days, came in at 6.23 percent for the week of April 23rd, the lowest reading at this point in the year in three spring buying seasons. Mortgage Bankers Association applications were up 7.9 percent the prior week. The setup for spring buyers is the best we've seen since 2023.

JENNY: But the warning we flagged last week was oil. Where did Brent finish?

ANDREW: Brent closed at 105.33 a barrel on Friday and was holding above 105 through the weekend. Early indications this morning have it pushing toward 107 as the Pakistan talks fall apart. We had said 103 was the level to watch for a mortgage-rate flip. We're now meaningfully above that. If oil holds here for another week, you should expect to see the 30-year drift back toward 6.5 percent, because the inflation read on the next CPI print gets harder for the bond market to ignore.

JENNY: So for someone trying to lock in a rate this week, what's the practical takeaway?

ANDREW: If you have a contract in hand, lock today. The downside risk on rates over the next two weeks is materially larger than the upside. The window we've been talking about all spring may be closing.

ANDREW: Jenny, your beat is having a moment. Let's go to AI, because the biggest deal in the industry happened Friday afternoon.

JENNY: It did. Google announced it will invest up to 40 billion dollars in Anthropic. The structure is 10 billion immediately at a 350-billion-dollar valuation, with another 30 billion contingent on Anthropic hitting performance milestones. Google Cloud will also deliver 5 gigawatts of compute starting in 2027. This comes just weeks after Amazon committed up to 25 billion dollars to the same company.

ANDREW: So Anthropic now has Amazon and Google both as massive backers. How is that not a conflict?

JENNY: It's the defining tension of this moment in AI. Google has its own frontier model, Gemini, which directly competes with Anthropic's Claude. Amazon has its own model line too. Both companies have decided that hedging on Anthropic, and capturing the cloud compute revenue Anthropic spends, is more valuable than treating it as pure competition. Anthropic's annual run-rate revenue just crossed 30 billion dollars, up from about 9 billion at the end of last year. That's the number that's making this make sense.

ANDREW: 30 billion in run-rate revenue at a 350-billion valuation. That's a much more reasonable multiple than the headline number suggests.

JENNY: Right. And it's why this isn't a vanity check. The other AI development worth flagging from the weekend is DeepSeek V4, the Chinese open-source model released Friday. It supports a 1-million-token native context window, and the inference cost is roughly one-sixth of the leading closed models. Open-source frontier AI has now closed most of the capability gap with the closed labs. That's a meaningful pressure point for OpenAI and Anthropic on pricing.

ANDREW: One-sixth the inference cost. That's the kind of number that changes enterprise procurement conversations.

JENNY: It does. Expect to see large customers using DeepSeek as leverage in their next renewal cycles, even if they don't actually deploy it. The other dynamic to watch is regulatory. The Treasury and Commerce departments have been quietly updating guidance about Chinese-origin AI models running in U.S. enterprise environments. A 1-million-token open-source model from a Chinese lab is exactly the test case those rules were designed for. Banks and law firms are going to be the most cautious adopters.

ANDREW: And on the home-grown side, Anthropic's own commercial reputation is still recovering from the Claude Code regression postmortem they published earlier this month. Three separate code-quality issues across March and April. Have enterprise customers moved on?

JENNY: Mostly yes, based on what I'm hearing. The transparency of that postmortem actually helped. But the Google deal is the bigger signal — when your largest competitor is also writing you a 40-billion-dollar check, the procurement teams stop worrying about platform risk.

JENNY: Andrew, before we get to Jacksonville, let's circle back to Iran for a beat, because that's the story tying everything together.

ANDREW: Iran is the swing variable on rates, on oil, on equities, on everything we've talked about this morning. Saturday, Trump canceled the planned trip by Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to Islamabad, where they were supposed to hold indirect talks with Iran's foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi. Trump said the U.S. received a deal offer from Iran that, quote, should have been better. Araghchi had left Pakistan about an hour before Trump pulled the plug.

JENNY: So is the ceasefire dead?

ANDREW: Trump told Axios it doesn't mean a return to active hostilities, but the U.S. naval blockade in the Strait of Hormuz is still in place, Iran's restrictions on shipping are still in place, and now there's no diplomatic track. The picture matches what Goldman Sachs is calling, quote, maritime trench warfare. Brent above 105, equities futures lower this morning, and a nervous bond market.

JENNY: And the Israel-Lebanon piece of this?

ANDREW: That ceasefire was extended three weeks last Thursday, so it runs through roughly mid-May. The original ten-day deal expired Sunday. Lebanon wanted a full month and got three weeks. Trump has floated hosting Netanyahu and Aoun at the White House, but no date has been set. So one track is holding, the other is breaking. The combined effect on oil is what's showing up in your gasoline price this week.

JENNY: That's the through-line of the whole show today, really. Let's bring it home to Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high in the mid-80s today with scattered showers and thunderstorms moving in this afternoon. Rain chances are around 80 percent. Good news for the wildfire crews. Bad news if you're trying to get a haircut at lunch.

JENNY: And speaking of wildfires, the Railroad Fire in Clay and Putnam Counties is now 70 percent contained, up from 65 percent on Thursday. The fire grew slightly to 4,743 acres over the weekend, but containment lines are holding. St. Lucie County sent a fire crew to assist on Saturday. The countywide burn ban in Duval is still in effect, and St. Johns County extended its burn ban through today.

ANDREW: Any timetable on full containment?

JENNY: Officials have been careful not to commit. The drought conditions across Northeast Florida are the worst since 1872, and any wind shift could reburn ground that's already been cleared. The honest answer is that this fire is going to smolder for weeks even after they call it contained.

JENNY: The other Jacksonville story is on the city council agenda tomorrow. The 12-million-dollar incentive package to keep the Winn-Dixie headquarters in Jacksonville is up for introduction at Tuesday night's meeting. That's 5.5 million in an enhanced value grant plus 6.5 million in a headquarters retention grant. It cleared the Mayor's Budget Review Committee unanimously on April 13th. The full council debate is the test.

ANDREW: Any organized opposition lining up?

JENNY: Not visibly yet, but the politics are interesting. This is an Aldi-owned chain getting taxpayer money to stay in a market it was already in, and the Jax Daily Record reporting suggests some council members are uncomfortable with the precedent. Watch for that argument to surface in committee.

JENNY: And one more local item with national interest. The Jaguars confirmed this past week that EverBank Stadium capacity for the 2026 season will be 42,507, down from 68,300. The entire upper deck — the 400 level — is closed for the season as construction on the 1.4-billion-dollar Stadium of the Future ramps up. About 22,000 of the displaced season ticket holders are getting first crack at lower-bowl seats. The team will play just six regular season home games in Jacksonville this fall, plus two designated home games in London at Wembley and Tottenham.

ANDREW: Six home games in your home city for a 1.4-billion-dollar publicly assisted stadium project. That's the trade.

JENNY: It is. The Washington Post editorial board called the deal a fleecing earlier this month after Orlando's competing pitch surfaced. The local counter is that 2027 is the truly painful year, when the Jaguars play none of their home games in Jacksonville. The economic impact study the city commissioned assumes that one-year hit is recoverable. We'll find out.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week: the Senate Banking Committee vote on Kevin Warsh on Wednesday. If it advances cleanly with all 13 Republicans in favor, the path to a floor vote before Powell's term ends on May 15th is real. If even one Republican defects after Tillis blessed the move, the timeline slips and we're back to the Vice Chair Jefferson interim scenario. The bigger question is what Warsh's committee vote does to the bond market that same afternoon, especially with oil where it is.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Monday. Have a great week.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Lebanon Ceasefire Extended, GPT-5.5 Arrives, Mortgage Rates Hit Spring Low
item.description: Israel and Lebanon extend their ceasefire three weeks as Hormuz tensions simmer. OpenAI drops GPT-5.5 just six weeks after 5.4. Mortgage rates slip to the cheapest spring in three years.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Friday, April 24th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Breaking overnight — Israel and Lebanon have agreed to extend their ceasefire by three weeks, announced by President Trump after a second round of talks at the White House. We'll walk through where that leaves the bigger Iran picture.

ANDREW: And OpenAI dropped GPT-5.5 yesterday, just six weeks after GPT-5.4. The cadence of these releases is starting to tell its own story about the AI race.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at 7,108, down about four-tenths of a percent. The Dow gave back 180 points to finish at 49,310. The Nasdaq was the bigger loser, down nearly nine-tenths to close at 24,438. It was the worst session of the week for the Nasdaq, as software stocks sold off and oil climbed on renewed Iran worries. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at about 4.3 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking around 6.23 percent according to Freddie Mac's weekly survey — more on that in a minute.

ANDREW: Jenny, let's start at the White House, because the national desk has a lot moving this morning.

JENNY: Go ahead.

ANDREW: After a second round of direct talks in the Oval Office yesterday, President Trump announced that Israel and Lebanon have agreed to extend their ceasefire by three weeks. The original 10-day deal was set to expire Sunday. Lebanon had wanted a full month — they didn't get that, but three weeks gives U.S. envoys room to try and turn a fragile pause into something closer to an actual peace framework.

JENNY: And this is the one you've been flagging as load-bearing for the bigger Iran picture, right?

ANDREW: Exactly. Washington wants Israel-Lebanon stable for two reasons. One, to try and advance direct peace talks — Trump said he plans to host Prime Minister Netanyahu and President Aoun at the White House in the near future. And two, to keep a second front from blowing up while the administration tries to manage the much messier situation in the Strait of Hormuz.

JENNY: Where things are not getting better.

ANDREW: They're not. U.S. Central Command now says the naval blockade of Iranian ports has turned around 31 vessels. Iran's Revolutionary Guard attacked three ships earlier this week and seized two of them. And the president has ordered the U.S. military to — his words — shoot and kill any Iranian boats laying mines in the strait. Brent crude is above 101 dollars a barrel. The ceasefire with Iran is technically still in place, but it is under real pressure.

JENNY: And on the Fed — any movement on Warsh?

ANDREW: Essentially none, and that's the story. Kevin Warsh had his confirmation hearing Monday. He vowed independence. But Senator Thom Tillis is still drawing a red line: he won't vote to advance Warsh out of the Banking Committee until the Justice Department drops its criminal investigation into current Fed chair Jerome Powell. Republicans only have a 12-to-10 edge on that committee, so one "no" blocks him. The soonest anyone sees a floor vote is the week of May 11th — and Powell's term expires May 15th.

JENNY: That is a very thin margin.

ANDREW: Four days between a possible Warsh vote and the Powell deadline. If the timing slips even slightly, Vice Chair Philip Jefferson would become acting chair. Which would be a first for the modern Fed.

JENNY: Let's shift to AI — because yesterday was a big day on two fronts. Andrew, I want your ear on this one, because of what it means for enterprise adoption.

ANDREW: Go ahead.

JENNY: OpenAI released GPT-5.5 yesterday, and the thing that jumped out isn't the benchmarks, which are strong. It's that this release came just six weeks after GPT-5.4. That is an unusually fast turn. OpenAI calls it their smartest and most intuitive model. It scored 88.7 percent on the main software-engineering coding benchmark enterprises actually watch, and the company claims a 60 percent drop in hallucinations versus 5.4.

ANDREW: So what does a six-week cadence tell you? Is that pressure, or is that confidence?

JENNY: A bit of both. Google shipped Gemini 3.1 Pro earlier this month. Anthropic has Claude Opus 4.5. Nobody wants to leave a frontier model out in the market for longer than a quarter anymore. The cost to enterprises is they're now evaluating new models every six to eight weeks instead of every six months. That changes procurement cycles.

JENNY: Speaking of Anthropic — they put out a postmortem yesterday that I think explains a lot of the grumbling engineering teams have had in April. Anthropic admitted three separate issues between March and April made Claude Code measurably worse. A reduction in default reasoning from high to medium to fight interface lag. A caching bug that kept wiping the model's short-term memory mid-session. And a verbosity instruction added April 16th that told the model to keep responses under 100 words — which dropped coding quality by 3 percent in their own evaluations.

ANDREW: Three percent sounds small until you're the developer debugging it.

JENNY: That's exactly right. All three have now been reverted, and Anthropic reset usage limits for subscribers yesterday as a concession. The broader lesson for enterprise AI buyers: model quality is not a fixed property. A vendor can silently change behavior in ways you wouldn't catch without your own evaluations running.

JENNY: And one more — Google used its Cloud Next conference this week to roll out its Ironwood chip to general availability and preview an eighth-generation design split into separate training and inference chips. Google is making a serious play to get customers off Nvidia for inference workloads.

ANDREW: Jenny, over to the home lending side. And there is actually good news to lead with.

JENNY: Please.

ANDREW: Mortgage rates fell again this week. Freddie Mac's weekly survey, released yesterday, puts the 30-year fixed at 6.23 percent, down from 6.30 a week ago. According to Freddie, that is the lowest reading at this point in the year in three spring homebuying seasons. And the Mortgage Bankers Association reported applications jumped 7.9 percent for the week ending April 17th — purchases and refinances both rose.

JENNY: So what is actually driving the move? Because the backdrop doesn't look friendly — oil is up, inflation worries are back.

ANDREW: Two forces are pulling on each other. On one side, safe-haven flows into Treasuries because of the Iran situation — that pushes yields and mortgage rates down. On the other side, higher oil feeds into inflation expectations, which pushes rates up. This week, the safe-haven trade won. The ten-year Treasury yield is at roughly 4.3 percent, and that is what's letting mortgage rates drift lower.

JENNY: So is this the window buyers have been waiting for?

ANDREW: For anyone sitting on a preapproval, this is a better rate environment than they have seen since last fall. I'd be cautious about calling it a sustainable move, though. If Hormuz escalates and oil goes higher, the inflation story flips the trade within a week. Buyers acting now are essentially making a bet that the safe-haven flow holds.

ANDREW: Jenny, over to you for Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high near 85 degrees today, mostly sunny skies, overnight low around 63. And that brings me straight to the story that isn't going away.

JENNY: The Railroad Fire in Clay and Putnam counties is now holding at 4,406 acres and reached 65 percent containment yesterday, up from 55 percent earlier in the week. That's progress. But most of Northeast Florida is still under a red flag warning — strong winds, warm temperatures, and drought conditions that officials are calling the worst Jacksonville has seen since 1872. The countywide burn ban Duval County put in place earlier this week remains in effect.

ANDREW: Is there any rain in the forecast?

JENNY: Not in a useful window. Forecasters are saying fire conditions could persist for weeks. Which is why I want to flag two business stories happening against that backdrop, because life is going on in Jacksonville.

JENNY: Harris Teeter broke ground this morning on a new store at 11901 Atlantic Boulevard. Mayor Donna Deegan and Harris Teeter President Tammy DeBoer were both at the ceremony. The store is targeting a spring 2027 opening. It is the chain's first meaningful push into North Florida, and another signal that grocery investment in the Atlantic corridor is not slowing down.

JENNY: And a reminder for next week — the 12 million dollar incentive package to keep Winn-Dixie's headquarters in Jacksonville is scheduled for introduction at the City Council meeting on Tuesday, April 28th. The Jax Daily Record has been following this closely. It's a 5.5 million dollar enhanced value grant plus a 6.5 million dollar headquarters retention grant, tied to keeping Winn-Dixie at its offices on Edgewood Court.

ANDREW: And the JEA investigation?

JENNY: Still grinding forward. State Attorney Melissa Nelson's office has issued the broadest subpoena yet in this probe — records on CEO Vickie Cavey, Council President Kevin Carrico, and the lobbying firm Ballard Partners, which employs former Mayor Lenny Curry. No indictments. But News4Jax reported this week that the city quoted them nearly 4,000 dollars for the public records tied to the subpoena — which tells you something about how heavy this is getting on the ground.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today. It's Friday, which means the bond market closes at three. If the safe-haven flow into Treasuries holds through the session, the 30-year mortgage rate carries this week's improvement into Monday. If oil closes above 103 dollars on any fresh Hormuz news, the story flips — and the spring rate window Freddie is calling the best in three years could start closing fast. Watch the ten-year Treasury yield. If it is still anchored around 4.3 percent at the close, buyers have a weekend to get moving.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Friday. Have a great weekend.

ANDREW: We'll see you Monday.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Iran Fires on Ships in Hormuz Despite Ceasefire, Markets Hit Records
item.description: Iran's Revolutionary Guard attacks three commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz hours after Trump extends the ceasefire. Wall Street closes at record highs. Google says seventy-five percent of its new code is now AI-generated.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Thursday, April twenty-third, twenty twenty-six. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Iran fired on three commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz yesterday, seizing two of them, just hours after President Trump extended the ceasefire. We'll break down what that means for the blockade standoff, oil prices, and a region that cannot seem to find its footing.

ANDREW: And Google's CEO dropped a remarkable number this week. Three out of every four lines of new code at the company are now written by artificial intelligence. We'll unpack what that means for the rest of the industry.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at seven thousand one hundred thirty-eight, up about one percent. The Dow gained three hundred forty-one points to close just under forty-nine thousand five hundred. The Nasdaq jumped one point six percent to twenty-four thousand six hundred fifty-eight. All three hit fresh record highs on Wednesday, fueled in part by the ceasefire extension. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at four point three one percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at roughly six point two percent according to Optimal Blue. One more note. Tesla reported first-quarter earnings after the bell. Revenue came in at twenty-two point four billion dollars, up sixteen percent year over year. Earnings beat expectations, but the stock gave up early gains after the company said capital spending this year will top twenty-five billion, five billion more than previously guided.

ANDREW: Let's start with the story that's driving everything else this morning, the Strait of Hormuz.

ANDREW: Updating a story we've been tracking all week. President Trump extended the Iran ceasefire indefinitely on Monday, conditional on Tehran submitting what he called a unified proposal. But the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports remained in place, and Iran has been clear that it considers the blockade itself a violation of the truce. Yesterday, they acted on that. Iran's Revolutionary Guard fired on three commercial vessels transiting the strait and seized two of them, the Italian-owned MSC Francesca and a vessel called the Epaminondas. PBS, NBC, and CNBC all confirmed the attacks. The Pentagon is now sending additional forces to the region.

JENNY: So the ceasefire is technically still in effect, but Iran is treating the blockade as a separate fight?

ANDREW: That's exactly the tension. Tehran says the blockade is an act of war and until it's lifted, they won't come back to the table. Washington says the blockade is a separate enforcement action, not covered by the ceasefire terms. Meanwhile, the strait has been effectively closed for about two months now. Brent crude jumped more than three percent on Wednesday to close above a hundred and one dollars a barrel, according to CNBC. For context, roughly twenty percent of the world's oil shipments normally transit that waterway. Every day this drags on, it feeds directly into global energy prices and inflation expectations.

JENNY: And there was a multinational planning conference in London this week?

ANDREW: Right. The UK and France hosted a thirty-plus nation military planning conference at Northwood on Tuesday, focused on a potential multinational effort to reopen the strait. No public details yet on outcomes, but the scale of that gathering, more than thirty countries at the table, tells you how seriously the international community is taking this bottleneck. The U.S. Central Command also confirmed it has redirected thirty-one vessels as part of the ongoing blockade enforcement, most of them oil tankers.

ANDREW: Two more stories from the national desk. First, Kevin Warsh's path to becoming the next Fed chair is still blocked. As we reported Tuesday, Warsh testified before the Senate Banking Committee on Monday and vowed he would not be, in his words, the president's sock puppet. He outlined plans for what he called regime change at the Fed, including fewer policy meetings, a new inflation framework, and a smaller balance sheet. But none of that matters right now because Republican Senator Thom Tillis is holding firm. He won't vote yes until the Justice Department drops its investigation of current Chair Jay Powell. Without Tillis, there aren't enough votes. CNN reported Wednesday that there's still a narrow procedural path to confirmation, but it would require either the DOJ backing down or a last-minute deal.

JENNY: And Powell's term expires when?

ANDREW: May fifteenth. That's twenty-two days away. If Warsh isn't confirmed by then, Vice Chair Philip Jefferson would step in as acting chair. That's not unprecedented, but it would add another layer of uncertainty for bond markets and raise fresh questions about the Fed's independence and credibility at a moment when inflation is still running above target.

ANDREW: Second, Israel and Lebanon resume direct talks in Washington today. This is only the second round of U.S.-brokered negotiations, and the clock is ticking. The ten-day ceasefire expires Saturday, April twenty-sixth. According to the Washington Post, Lebanon's delegation plans to request a one-month extension. But Hezbollah has publicly said it won't honor any agreements that come out of these talks, which complicates things considerably. The broader goal is a permanent cessation of hostilities, Israeli troop withdrawal from southern Lebanon, prisoner releases, and the start of reconstruction.

JENNY: A lot riding on those talks. Andrew, let me jump over to Jacksonville before we get to home lending.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at eighty-one degrees and mostly sunny today. But do not let the sunshine fool you. Fire danger remains extreme across the region.

JENNY: Updating the Railroad Fire we've been following since Sunday. The blaze has now burned more than four thousand one hundred acres across Clay and Putnam Counties and is about fifty-five percent contained, according to the Florida Forest Service. That's up from fifty percent on Tuesday, so crews are making progress, but it's slow going. The Florida National Guard has mobilized six helicopters to assist, and voluntary evacuations remain in place in Green Cove Springs and Bostwick. U.S. Route 17 is still closed through the fire zone.

ANDREW: And the drought driving all of this isn't going anywhere soon?

JENNY: No, and that's the alarming part. Florida's Agriculture Commissioner issued an urgent warning this week. Northeast Florida is in what's classified as exceptional drought, the most severe category on the federal scale. Fire officials told Action News Jax that conditions could persist for another ten weeks without substantial rainfall. Jacksonville Fire and Rescue responded by imposing a countywide burn ban across all of Duval County, effective immediately. No open burning of any kind until further notice. And officials are warning that even with containment improving on the Railroad Fire, the underlying conditions mean new fires could spark at any time. Smoke from the fire drifted into Duval County early yesterday morning, and air quality advisories remain in effect across several Northeast Florida counties.

JENNY: On the JEA front, the investigation we've been tracking continues to widen. The City Council's special investigatory committee is weighing whether to expand its scope beyond workplace culture allegations to include JEA's lobbying contracts and a major power plant project. The State Attorney's subpoenas cover CEO Vickie Cavey, Council President Kevin Carrico, and Ballard Partners, the lobbying firm that employs former Mayor Lenny Curry. No indictments yet, but the public records controversy we mentioned earlier this week is adding fuel. News4Jax reported the city quoted them nearly four thousand dollars just to fulfill a records request related to the subpoenas.

JENNY: Andrew, what are you seeing on the mortgage side?

ANDREW: It was a relatively quiet twenty-four hours for home lending. The thirty-year fixed rate is sitting at roughly six point two percent according to Optimal Blue's daily index. Freddie Mac's weekly average from last Thursday came in at six point three percent, and daily trackers this week have shown rates holding steady to slightly lower. No major regulatory announcements and no fresh data from the MBA on applications yet.

ANDREW: The bigger picture here is what we've been calling the geopolitical rate window. When Middle East tensions flare, investors buy Treasuries as a safe haven, which pushes yields down and takes mortgage rates along for the ride. That dynamic helped pull rates from about six point four percent a month ago down to where they sit now. But here's the catch. If Iran's Hormuz escalation keeps oil above a hundred dollars for any extended period, that feeds directly into inflation expectations, which pushes rates in the opposite direction.

JENNY: So borrowers are caught between two competing forces.

ANDREW: Exactly. If a diplomatic deal materializes and the strait reopens, the safe-haven trade unwinds and rates tick up. If the conflict deepens and oil stays elevated, inflation expectations do the same thing from the other side. Either way, this low-six-percent range may not hold much longer. If you're rate-locked, that's good news. If you're still shopping, the window may be narrower than it looks.

JENNY: Andrew, let me take us to technology.

JENNY: The headline number this week comes from Google. CEO Sundar Pichai disclosed at Google Cloud Next that seventy-five percent of all new code at Google is now AI-generated and then reviewed and approved by human engineers. That's up from fifty percent just last fall. Pichai also said that agent-assisted workflows completed a complex code migration six times faster than was possible a year ago with engineers working alone.

ANDREW: And they're making this effectively mandatory for engineering teams?

JENNY: Increasingly, yes. Google is factoring AI tool adoption into some engineers' performance reviews, which is a pretty clear signal that this isn't optional. They're even allowing select teams to use third-party tools like Claude Code alongside Google's own Gemini models. The broader takeaway is that AI coding has moved from experiment to expectation at the biggest tech companies. The question now is how fast the rest of the industry follows.

JENNY: And speaking of the AI economy more broadly, a new PwC study puts some hard numbers on the divide. They surveyed more than twelve hundred senior executives across twenty-five sectors and found that just twenty percent of companies are capturing roughly seventy-four percent of AI's total economic value. Those leaders are generating more than seven times the AI-driven revenue and efficiency gains of the average competitor. The difference, according to PwC, isn't the technology itself. It's strategy. The top performers are using AI for growth and new revenue lines, not just cutting costs.

ANDREW: So the gap is widening.

JENNY: Rapidly. And PwC found that the leading companies are nearly twice as likely to be deploying AI in autonomous, self-optimizing ways rather than just simple task automation. The implication for enterprises is pretty stark. If you're only using AI to trim expenses, you may already be falling behind.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today and into the weekend. And a quick note for football fans, the NFL Draft kicks off tonight from Pittsburgh, first round at eight Eastern.

ANDREW: But the bigger thing to watch is diplomacy. Israel and Lebanon sit down in Washington this morning for that second round of direct talks. The ceasefire expires Saturday. If Lebanon secures the one-month extension it's requesting, that buys time for a more durable agreement. If the talks stall, and Hezbollah's public opposition suggests they might, fighting could resume by Sunday. Watch for statements from the State Department later today. The outcome will ripple through oil markets, defense stocks, and the broader Middle East calculus heading into next week.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Thursday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Trump Extends Iran Ceasefire but Keeps Blockade, Apple Names Ternus as Next CEO
item.description: Trump extends the Iran ceasefire indefinitely but the naval blockade stays. Tim Cook hands Apple to hardware chief John Ternus. Warsh tells senators he won't be a "sock puppet." Railroad Fire hits 50 percent containment.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Wednesday, April twenty-second, twenty-twenty-six. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: The Iran ceasefire was set to expire tonight, but President Trump extended it late last evening with one major catch: the naval blockade of Iranian ports stays in place, and Tehran is calling that a dealbreaker.

ANDREW: And a major leadership change at the world's most valuable company. Tim Cook is stepping aside as Apple's CEO. Hardware chief John Ternus will take over September first. We'll get into what that means for Apple's AI future.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed yesterday at seven thousand sixty-four, down about six tenths of a percent. The Dow shed two hundred ninety-three points, also down six tenths, finishing at forty-nine thousand one hundred forty-nine. The Nasdaq settled at twenty-four thousand two hundred sixty, down a similar margin. It was a cautious session driven largely by uncertainty over the Iran ceasefire. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at four point two-five percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is at six point three percent, according to Freddie Mac. More on rates in a few minutes.

ANDREW: Let's start with the story that had investors on edge all day. Updating the Iran conflict we've been tracking: the two-week ceasefire was set to expire tonight, but late last evening President Trump posted that he would extend it. He cited what he called a "seriously fractured" Iranian government and said Pakistani mediators, specifically Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif and Field Marshal Asim Munir, asked him to hold off on resuming strikes until Tehran's leaders can present a unified proposal to end the war.

JENNY: So how long does the extension last?

ANDREW: It's open-ended, which sounds reassuring on the surface. But the terms are loaded. Trump said the ceasefire continues until Iran submits a unified proposal. There's no deadline. But the naval blockade of Iranian ports stays fully in place, and that's the problem. Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araghchi has called the blockade an "act of war" and says it violates the spirit of the ceasefire. Iran's United Nations envoy said flatly that the next round of talks won't happen until Washington lifts it. So we have a ceasefire without a pathway back to the negotiating table.

JENNY: And the Strait of Hormuz is still effectively closed. It's been almost two months now, right?

ANDREW: That's right. And today in London, we're seeing the first concrete step toward planning a reopening. The UK and France are hosting military planners from more than thirty nations at the British Permanent Joint Headquarters at Northwood. This is the first time the conversation has moved from diplomacy to actual operational planning, working out command and control, force deployment, and capabilities. It follows a diplomatic summit in Paris last week that brought together fifty-one countries. Brent crude is still trading in the mid-to-upper nineties per barrel, and oil markets will be watching this conference closely.

JENNY: What about the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire? That's also on a clock.

ANDREW: It is. That ten-day ceasefire is now in its sixth day, set to expire April twenty-sixth. Both sides have accused each other of violations, and Lebanon reports Israeli demolitions inside the disputed buffer zone. But there is a diplomatic opening. Direct talks between Israeli and Lebanese representatives are scheduled for Thursday in Washington. And Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam met with President Macron in Paris yesterday, requesting five hundred million euros in humanitarian aid over the next six months. So there's movement, but the window is narrow.

ANDREW: Sticking with the national beat, let's talk about what happened on Capitol Hill yesterday. We reported on Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing for the Fed chair job, and the headlines came fast. When a senator asked point-blank whether he'd be President Trump's "sock puppet," Warsh answered, quote, "absolutely not." He said the president never asked him to commit to any specific interest rate decision. But Warsh also outlined what he's calling a regime change at the Fed, potentially fewer policy meetings per year, a new framework for how the Fed targets inflation, and a push to reduce the Fed's six point seven trillion dollar balance sheet, which he argued has contributed to wealth inequality.

JENNY: Strong performance. But the math still doesn't work for him, does it?

ANDREW: Not yet. Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina is still blocking the vote. Tillis says he won't move on any Fed nominee until the Justice Department drops what he calls a "frivolous" investigation into current Chair Jay Powell. And with Powell's term expiring May fifteenth, the clock is very real. If Warsh isn't confirmed by then, we could see a gap in Fed leadership at a moment when markets are already jittery from the Iran situation and oil prices.

JENNY: Andrew, with all of this geopolitical uncertainty, what does it mean for the mortgage market right now?

ANDREW: Good question. The thirty-year fixed is at six point three percent this week, down from six point three-seven the prior week, according to Freddie Mac's Primary Mortgage Market Survey. We've been tracking a steady decline over the past week or so, and the driver is pretty straightforward. When investors get nervous about geopolitics, they move money into safe-haven assets like Treasury bonds. That pushes yields down, and mortgage rates follow. So ironically, the Iran war tensions have been creating a modest window for homebuyers.

JENNY: So a war dividend for mortgage shoppers? That feels fragile.

ANDREW: It is fragile. If Trump's ceasefire extension holds and the Hormuz situation stabilizes, that safe-haven trade could unwind quickly. Yields would rise, and rates would follow them right back up. We actually saw a small uptick yesterday on stronger-than-expected employment data, so the floor isn't firm. And with Brent crude in the mid-nineties, inflation expectations aren't going away anytime soon. For buyers who've been watching rates tick down toward six percent, this may be a narrow window. The MBA's weekly mortgage application data, which will be out later this week, should tell us whether borrowers are jumping on it.

ANDREW: And one note on the MBA's weekly application data. When it drops later this week, watch whether purchase applications are rising. That's the clearest signal of whether everyday buyers are actually taking advantage of this rate dip or whether the geopolitical uncertainty is keeping them on the sidelines.

JENNY: Good context. Let me bring us to Jacksonville. Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at seventy-nine degrees and partly sunny today, but the bigger story remains the drought. Most of northeast Florida is under exceptional drought conditions, the worst classification, and a red flag warning is in effect again today with dry fuels, low humidity, and gusty east winds.

JENNY: And directly related to that, an update on a story we've been following all week. The Railroad Fire burning along the Clay-Putnam county line has grown to just over four thousand one hundred acres, but here's the encouraging news: containment jumped from ten percent to fifty percent as of Tuesday afternoon. That's a significant leap. The Florida Forest Service brought in three air tankers and two scooper aircraft for retardant and water drops, and crews worked through the night establishing fire break lines.

ANDREW: From ten to fifty percent in about twenty-four hours? That's impressive progress given how dry it's been.

JENNY: It really is. Voluntary evacuations are still in place for parts of Green Cove Springs and north of Bostwick, and US seventeen remains closed at the Clay-Putnam line. But the trend is heading the right direction. Officials are warning, though, that this fire season is far from over. Northeast Florida hasn't had significant rain in weeks, and the conditions that sparked this blaze, literally from a passing train, show just how combustible the landscape is right now.

JENNY: Also in Jacksonville, the JEA investigation continues to expand. As we've been reporting, there are now four parallel investigations into the city-owned utility. State Attorney Melissa Nelson's subpoena targets CEO Vickie Cavey, Council President Kevin Carrico, and communications with Ballard Partners, the lobbying firm that employs former Mayor Lenny Curry. The subpoena is seeking emails, texts, and records containing some pointed keywords. Meanwhile, the City Council's investigatory committee is weighing whether to broaden its own probe to include a lobbying contract and a power plant project. No indictments, but the scope keeps growing.

ANDREW: And the city quoted a news outlet nearly four thousand dollars for public records related to this?

JENNY: That's right. News4Jax asked for records related to Carrico's communications, and the city came back with a price tag of almost four thousand dollars. The cost itself has become part of the transparency debate. No indictments yet, but this story has a lot of threads, and none of them seem to be wrapping up anytime soon.

JENNY: Now let's talk tech. Andrew, you mentioned Apple in the open, and this really is a landmark moment. Tim Cook announced he'll step down as CEO on September first after fifteen years in the role. His successor is John Ternus, Apple's senior vice president of hardware engineering. Ternus has been with Apple for twenty-five years and led development on iPads, AirPods, and recent iPhones. Cook will move into the role of executive chairman.

ANDREW: The timing is interesting. Why now?

JENNY: A few things. Cook is sixty-five and has been talking about succession for a while. But the bigger signal, according to Bloomberg, is that Apple's board sees Ternus as someone who can bring back the product-focused decisiveness of the Steve Jobs era. Apple's AI strategy has been widely criticized as lagging behind competitors. Siri still frustrates users. Apple Intelligence has been slow to roll out. Ternus is a hardware guy, but the bet is that he can bring the same discipline to Apple's AI push. Cook isn't vanishing. He'll stay on as chairman working with policymakers globally. But make no mistake, this is Apple admitting it needs a new direction.

JENNY: And one more from the AI beat, and this one is about the sheer scale of money flowing into the space right now. Amazon deepened its partnership with Anthropic in a major way over the weekend. Amazon is investing an additional five billion dollars immediately in Anthropic, the maker of Claude, with up to twenty billion more tied to commercial milestones. According to Bloomberg and CNBC, the deal values Anthropic at three hundred fifty billion dollars.

ANDREW: What does Anthropic commit to in return?

JENNY: More than one hundred billion dollars in Amazon Web Services spending over the next decade. That includes using up to five gigawatts of compute capacity running on Amazon's custom Trainium chips. It's essentially a mutual lock-in. Amazon becomes Anthropic's default infrastructure provider, and Anthropic gives AWS the anchor tenant it needs to compete with Microsoft's OpenAI relationship. This brings Amazon's total investment in Anthropic to as much as thirty-three billion. The AI infrastructure arms race is accelerating, and the checks keep getting bigger.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today. That London conference at Northwood. If military planners from thirty-plus nations can agree on an operational framework for reopening the Strait of Hormuz, it signals that the international community is preparing for a post-ceasefire reality, even if diplomacy between Washington and Tehran is stalled. Any concrete progress could ease oil markets and, by extension, put upward pressure on Treasury yields and mortgage rates. Any breakdown, or signs that the coalition can't agree, does the opposite. Either way, this meeting matters for your portfolio and your mortgage.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Wednesday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Warsh Faces Senate on Fed Independence, Iran Ceasefire Clock Ticking
item.description: Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh testifies before the Senate today with Fed independence the central question. Iran pulls out of peace talks after the Touska seizure, and a 3,000-acre wildfire forces evacuations near Jacksonville.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Tuesday, April twenty-first, twenty twenty-six. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: All eyes are on Capitol Hill this morning, where Kevin Warsh sits down in front of the Senate Banking Committee for his Fed chair confirmation hearing, and the question everyone wants answered is whether he'll stand up to the White House on interest rates.

ANDREW: And with the Iran ceasefire set to expire tomorrow night and Tehran pulling out of peace talks, markets are bracing for a volatile stretch. Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed yesterday at 7,109, down about a quarter of a percent. The Dow barely moved, finishing at 49,443, essentially flat. The Nasdaq slipped a quarter percent to 24,404, snapping its thirteen-day winning streak, the longest since 1992. It was a risk-off session driven by the Iran ship seizure over the weekend. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.25 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at 6.19 percent according to Freddie Mac's latest survey. Oil told the bigger story yesterday. Brent crude jumped five percent to 95 dollars a barrel.

JENNY: That oil move is significant. Andrew, let's start with this Warsh hearing because it could shape monetary policy for years.

ANDREW: It really could. Kevin Warsh sits down in front of the Senate Banking Committee at ten o'clock this morning for the most consequential Fed confirmation hearing in a generation. Warsh is President Trump's pick to replace Jerome Powell, whose term as chair expires May fifteenth. In prepared testimony released last night, Warsh said the Fed must, quote, stay in its lane, and pledged that the conduct of monetary policy will remain strictly independent.

JENNY: That sounds reassuring on the surface, but there's a catch, right?

ANDREW: There's a big catch. That "stay in its lane" language cuts two ways. Warsh is using it to argue the Fed overstepped by researching climate economics and targeting inclusive employment. He's essentially saying independence applies to rate-setting, but in other areas the Fed should be more accountable to the White House and Congress. Democrats on the committee are going to press him hard on whether that distinction creates a backdoor for political pressure on monetary policy. And remember, Trump has explicitly threatened to force Powell out.

JENNY: And there's the Tillis problem we've been tracking.

ANDREW: Right. Senator Thom Tillis, Republican from North Carolina, is still blocking the confirmation. He told NBC News last week, quote, I will vote for Kevin Warsh simultaneously with the conclusion of the DOJ investigation, and not a day before. That investigation is a probe into cost overruns at the Fed's headquarters renovation, and it appears aimed at Powell. Republicans have a slim majority on the Banking Committee, so without Tillis, Warsh cannot clear the committee. Watch for whether Tillis softens his stance after today's testimony, because that's the real bottleneck.

JENNY: So even a strong performance today doesn't guarantee he gets through.

ANDREW: Not even close. Now, the other story that's moving markets this morning. Updating a story we've been tracking all week, Iran has formally pulled out of the Pakistan-hosted peace talks after the U.S. Navy seized the Iranian cargo ship Touska in the Gulf of Oman on Saturday. U.S. Marines boarded the vessel after what CNBC reported was a six-hour standoff. Iran's Foreign Ministry called the seizure an act of piracy and said it has no plans to send negotiators to Islamabad. The Iranian military has vowed retaliation.

JENNY: And the ceasefire clock is ticking. Where does Vice President Vance's diplomatic effort stand?

ANDREW: Vance was already headed to Pakistan for a second round of talks, but without Iran at the table, there's no one to negotiate with. President Trump said the ceasefire expires Wednesday evening Washington time and called an extension, quote, highly unlikely without a deal. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to commercial shipping. That's why Brent crude spiked five percent to 95 dollars a barrel yesterday, and West Texas Intermediate crossed 86 dollars. Energy traders are pricing in the real possibility that this falls apart.

JENNY: What happens to oil if the ceasefire actually lapses?

ANDREW: If there's no diplomatic path by tomorrow night, analysts expect resumed U.S. and Israeli strikes and oil prices pushing well past 100 dollars a barrel. That would ripple straight through to gas prices and inflation expectations, which is exactly the backdrop the Fed does not want heading into a leadership transition.

JENNY: Meanwhile, the separate Israel-Lebanon ceasefire that began April sixteenth is holding, at least on paper. Lebanon's army has reported Israeli demolitions, artillery shelling, and land-clearing operations inside a disputed zone that Israel calls the Yellow Line, roughly ten kilometers inside southern Lebanon. Hezbollah and the Lebanese government have rejected it as occupation of sovereign territory. That ten-day truce expires April twenty-sixth.

ANDREW: A lot of deadlines converging this week. Jenny, what's happening closer to home in Jacksonville?

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at 75 degrees and sunny today, but it is breezy, with gusts up to 24 miles per hour, and that wind is not helping the fire situation.

JENNY: The big local story this morning is the Railroad Fire. Two wildfires near the Clay-Putnam County line merged over the weekend into a single blaze that has now burned more than 3,000 acres and is only ten percent contained, according to the Florida Forest Service. The fire is believed to have started from sparks off a train. Voluntary evacuations are underway in parts of Green Cove Springs and north of Bostwick, and the National Guard has been called in to help.

ANDREW: Three thousand acres at ten percent containment. What kind of resources are they throwing at this?

JENNY: News4Jax is reporting fifteen tractor-plow units on scene building firebreaks, plus two airtankers and two helicopters making retardant and water drops. The fire has been behaving erratically, spotting across U.S. 17. That highway is now shut down at the Clay-Putnam County line. And here's the ripple effect that goes beyond the region. Amtrak has suspended all rail service in and out of Florida because of visibility and safety concerns along the tracks. So if you're trying to take the Auto Train, it's not running.

ANDREW: That's a statewide impact from a local fire. Any sense of when containment might improve?

JENNY: Conditions are tough. Northeast Florida is in a drought, and these gusty winds are not cooperating. The Forest Service hasn't given a timeline for full containment. Shelters are open at the Bostwick Community Center in Palatka and Shiloh Baptist Church in Green Cove Springs for anyone who needs them.

JENNY: On a different front, the JEA investigation continues to grind forward. We reported last week on the State Attorney's subpoena. The latest development is that News4Jax requested public records related to Council President Kevin Carrico's communications tied to that subpoena, and the city quoted them nearly 4,000 dollars to produce those records. Meanwhile, City Council has delayed two JEA board appointments while investigations continue. No indictments yet, but the investigatory committee is considering expanding its scope to include JEA's lobbying contract and a major power plant project.

ANDREW: Sounds like that story is getting bigger, not smaller. And that four-thousand-dollar records request is worth watching. When a city makes it expensive to get public records, it tends to raise more questions than it answers.

JENNY: Agreed. Andrew, over to you for home lending.

ANDREW: Mortgage rates are on a six-day losing streak, and I mean that in a good way if you're shopping for a home. The thirty-year fixed has drifted down to roughly 6.19 percent, according to the latest survey data, with some lenders within striking distance of six percent flat. Freddie Mac's weekly survey pegged the average at 6.30 percent for the week ending April sixteenth, down from 6.37 the week before. That's a five-week low.

JENNY: What's driving that decline? Is it the same safe-haven trade we're seeing in Treasuries because of Iran?

ANDREW: Exactly right. When geopolitical risk spikes, investors pile into U.S. Treasuries, which pushes yields down, and mortgage rates follow the ten-year yield. With the ten-year sitting at 4.25 percent, there's room for rates to drift a bit lower. But here's the tension. If the Iran ceasefire collapses tomorrow night and oil spikes past 100 dollars, that feeds inflation expectations, which could reverse the whole trend. And the Warsh hearing today matters here too. If bond traders hear anything that suggests the Fed's independence is softer than they thought, yields could jump.

JENNY: So it's a window that could close quickly.

ANDREW: Very quickly. If you've been waiting for a better rate to lock, this week might be as good as it gets for a while. The MBA mortgage application data comes out later this week, and that'll tell us whether buyers are actually responding to these lower rates.

JENNY: Andrew, let me shift to the AI beat because Anthropic dropped something significant this month that's still making waves.

JENNY: Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview, which was announced on April seventh, is being called a turning point in cybersecurity. This is a model that, in controlled testing, could execute multi-stage attacks on networks and discover zero-day vulnerabilities autonomously, tasks that would take experienced human security professionals days to complete. The U.K.'s AI Safety Institute confirmed those findings in an independent evaluation. Anthropic restricted access to a handful of vetted organizations and launched something called Project Glasswing, a coalition with Microsoft, Google, Apple, Amazon, and JPMorgan Chase to use the model defensively.

ANDREW: So Anthropic built a model that's essentially a world-class hacker and then decided it was too dangerous to release broadly?

JENNY: That's the gist. Foreign Policy called it a shift in the cyber calculus. The model solved a thirty-two-step corporate network attack simulation end-to-end in three out of ten attempts. The NSA is reportedly already using it. The debate now is whether restricting access actually works long-term, or whether competitors will build something similar without the same guardrails.

ANDREW: That's the question that keeps security folks up at night.

JENNY: And one more from the AI world. PwC published its twenty twenty-six AI Performance Study, and the headline number is striking. Seventy-four percent of AI's economic value is being captured by just twenty percent of companies. The top performers are generating over seven times more AI-driven financial returns than the rest, with four percentage points higher profit margins. PwC surveyed more than twelve hundred senior executives across twenty-five sectors.

ANDREW: What separates the winners from everyone else?

JENNY: Strategy, mostly. The leaders are using AI to create entirely new revenue streams and rethink their business models. The majority of companies are still focused on automating what they already do, basically cost-cutting. PwC found that AI leaders are increasing the number of decisions made without human intervention at nearly three times the rate of their peers. It's a widening gap, and it has real implications for financial services firms in particular.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today and tomorrow. The Warsh hearing kicks off at ten a.m. Eastern, and the key moment will be when senators press him on whether a President Trump phone call about interest rates would constitute appropriate communication or political interference. His answer will move the bond market in real time. Then tomorrow evening, the Iran ceasefire expires. If Tehran doesn't return to the table by then, we're looking at a potential resumption of hostilities and an oil shock that would ripple through everything from gas prices to mortgage rates.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Tuesday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — US Seizes Iranian Ship, Ceasefire Expiring Wednesday, Warsh Faces Senate Tomorrow
item.description: The US Navy seized an Iranian cargo ship Saturday as the two-week ceasefire expires Wednesday. Kevin Warsh faces the Senate Banking Committee tomorrow. Mortgage rates hit a five-week low at 6.3 percent.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning, and welcome back from the weekend. It's Monday, April 20th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: The United States Navy fired on and seized an Iranian cargo ship over the weekend, and Tehran is now threatening retaliation. The two-week ceasefire expires Wednesday — with no deal in sight and fresh fighting words on both sides.

ANDREW: And Kevin Warsh sits before the Senate Banking Committee tomorrow morning for his confirmation hearing to become the next Federal Reserve chair. It is the most consequential monetary policy hearing in nearly a decade, and the outcome could move markets before the week is out.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets first. The S and P 500 closed Friday at roughly 7,126, up about one point two percent. The Dow finished near 49,450, up one point eight percent. The Nasdaq closed at around 24,470, up one and a half percent. It was a broadly positive week for equities, extending a two-week rally built on Middle East de-escalation hopes — though those hopes got complicated over the weekend. The ten-year Treasury yield settled Friday at four point two six percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is at six point three percent according to Freddie Mac's latest weekly survey — a five-week low for borrowers.

ANDREW: Before we get into the beats, a quick weekend at a glance. The single biggest development was Saturday's seizure of an Iranian cargo ship by the U.S. Navy in the Gulf of Oman. By Sunday, Iran's military had formally threatened retaliation, and Vice President JD Vance was on his way to Pakistan for what the White House is billing as a second round of direct talks — though Tehran has publicly said it has no plan to send anyone to the table.

ANDREW: Let's start with what drove all of it. The two-week ceasefire between the United States and Iran — brokered by Pakistan and signed on April 8th — was built on a straightforward bargain: the U.S. would pause strikes on Iranian infrastructure, and Iran would keep the Strait of Hormuz open to commercial traffic. That bargain has been eroding since almost the first day. The U.S. maintained its naval blockade of Iranian ports. Iran periodically re-closed the Strait. And then Saturday happened.

ANDREW: The U.S. Navy intercepted an Iranian-flagged cargo ship called the Touska in the Gulf of Oman. According to President Trump, the crew was given fair warning to stand down, refused, and the Navy fired on the vessel's engine room to stop it. The Touska is a sanctioned Iranian vessel. Iran immediately called the boarding an act of piracy and a ceasefire violation. By Sunday morning, the Iranian military had issued a formal warning of retaliation.

JENNY: So where does that leave the Wednesday deadline? Is there any real diplomatic path here?

ANDREW: That's the core question going into this week. The White House confirmed Sunday that Vice President Vance, along with special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, are heading back to Islamabad for a second round of talks. This is the same delegation that spent twenty-one hours in Pakistan two weeks ago and came home without a deal. The difference now is that Iran's Foreign Ministry has publicly said there is no plan for new talks. So the U.S. team is flying toward a table that the other side hasn't agreed to sit at.

JENNY: And what happens if Wednesday arrives with no extension?

ANDREW: The scenario the markets are pricing in — but not yet fully accounting for — is a resumption of U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iranian infrastructure. That means oil. The Strait of Hormuz handles roughly one-fifth of the world's crude. A full breakdown, with Iran keeping the Strait closed and hostilities resuming, almost certainly pushes oil back toward and possibly above a hundred dollars a barrel. That feeds directly into inflation, which puts the Fed in a bind. And there's another clock ticking: the War Powers Act sixty-day deadline hits around May 1st, which would require Trump to get congressional authorization to continue military operations. No administration has ever actually complied with that, but it adds legal and political pressure at an already volatile moment.

ANDREW: On a separate note, also from the weekend — President Trump signed an executive order Friday directing the FDA to accelerate its review of psychedelic therapies that have already received breakthrough drug designation. Ibogaine, which has shown real promise for treating opioid addiction and PTSD in veterans, is the most prominent example. The order tells the Department of Health and Human Services to fast-track research approvals and expand patient access. Veterans advocacy groups from both parties have been pushing for exactly this kind of action, which gives it unusual bipartisan footing — but it will still face the FDA's existing approval infrastructure, and that process does not move quickly.

JENNY: Andrew, before we get to home lending — the Warsh hearing tomorrow feels like it touches everything right now. Rates, Fed independence, inflation. What should our listeners actually be watching for?

ANDREW: Great framing. Kevin Warsh testifies at ten a.m. Eastern before the Senate Banking Committee. He's Trump's pick to replace Jerome Powell, whose term expires May 15th. This is not a low-stakes hearing.

ANDREW: The financial disclosure piece has drawn real attention. Warsh has reported assets valued between a hundred thirty-one million and two hundred nine million dollars — substantially more wealth than any previous Fed chair nominee, according to reporting from The Hill and Fortune. That includes stakes in SpaceX and in Polymarket, the prediction market platform. Senators will have pointed questions about both. The Fed chair's forward guidance moves global markets. If you have a stake in prediction markets, there are obvious conflict-of-interest questions that the committee is going to press.

JENNY: And Senator Tillis is still a complication.

ANDREW: He is. Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina has made clear he will not vote for any Fed chair nominee until the Justice Department drops its investigation into Powell and the Fed's multibillion-dollar headquarters renovation project. Tillis is a senior Republican on that committee. If he holds, Warsh could face a very thin margin — or could stall entirely. Watch whether Tillis gives any signal tomorrow that he is softening. That's the tell on whether Warsh's path to confirmation is clear.

JENNY: And Powell has been pretty unambiguous that he's not going anywhere voluntarily.

ANDREW: Very much so. Powell has said publicly he intends to serve out his term, and legal consensus is that the president cannot fire a Fed governor except for cause — which is a high and undefined standard. Trump has threatened on Fox Business to force Powell out if he does not leave when his term expires. That threat has rattled markets before. Tomorrow's hearing should clarify whether Warsh is willing to distance himself from that kind of pressure or whether he'll be seen as a political instrument. How he answers questions on Fed independence may matter as much as anything else he says.

JENNY: All right, let me pick this up from the rate side. Are mortgage rates feeling any of this volatility?

ANDREW: Favorably, for now. Freddie Mac's latest weekly average has the thirty-year fixed at six point three percent, which is the lowest in about five weeks. What's happening is that geopolitical uncertainty has been pushing money into Treasuries — investors treat them as a safe haven — which drives Treasury prices up and yields down. Mortgage rates tend to track the ten-year yield closely, and that yield has dropped from around four point six percent a month ago to four point two six percent as of Friday.

JENNY: So buyers who have been waiting are actually catching a window here?

ANDREW: They are, with a clear caveat. If the Iran ceasefire collapses Wednesday and oil spikes, you get an inflationary shock — and the Fed cannot cut rates into an inflationary shock. Rates could climb back quickly. For buyers in the market right now, the practical calculus this week is: if your loan is ready, locking before Wednesday's deadline might be worth the certainty. The MBA's mortgage application data comes out later this week, and that will tell us whether buyers are actually acting on the lower rates or still sitting on the sidelines.

ANDREW: One more wrinkle directly tied to tomorrow. If Warsh signals in his hearing that he would favor easier monetary policy — which some analysts expect — yields could fall further, which helps rates. If the hearing turns contentious, or if Tillis creates a visible standoff, you could see the opposite. Watch the ten-year yield in real time after the hearing opens. It is a live indicator of how the bond market is reading the testimony.

JENNY: Smart thing to watch. Okay, let me move to AI and technology — and the story of this week is about what agentic AI looks like when it actually goes to work.

ANDREW: What do you mean by that?

JENNY: EY — one of the Big Four accounting firms — has done something that genuinely stands apart from all the AI pilot programs we keep hearing about. They have deployed a multi-agent AI framework across their entire global assurance workforce. Not a test. Not a cohort. The whole workforce: a hundred thirty thousand auditing professionals, conducting a hundred sixty thousand audits in more than a hundred fifty countries. The system is built on Microsoft Azure and embedded directly into EY Canvas, their core audit platform.

ANDREW: And what are the agents actually doing?

JENNY: They're embedded in all phases of the audit workflow — sampling, documentation review, anomaly flagging, risk assessment — under human review at each stage. EY's framing is that auditors are being augmented, not replaced, and they have built a global retraining program to upskill the whole workforce. But when autonomous agents are embedded in the process for a hundred sixty thousand audits, the distinction between assistance and decision-making starts to compress. The accounting profession's regulators — the bodies that set audit standards — have not kept pace with how fast this technology has moved, and that gap is the risk that practitioners and clients will be watching.

JENNY: To put EY in context: OpenAI crossed twenty-five billion dollars in annualized revenue this month, according to CNBC, and Anthropic is approaching nineteen billion. Both companies are growing not primarily on consumer subscriptions now — they're growing on enterprise workforce deployments exactly like this one. The business model has shifted from selling AI access to embedding AI agents in how work actually gets done. EY's deployment is the most visible proof point yet that the shift is real and at scale.

ANDREW: So the question is: who sets the rules for those agents in a regulated profession?

JENNY: Exactly. Audit opinions carry legal weight. Financial statements rely on them. If an agent-assisted audit misses something material, the liability framework — which was written for human auditors — does not map cleanly onto an AI-assisted process. That is an open question that regulators, law firms, and the firms themselves are going to be wrestling with for the next several years.

JENNY: Let's head to Jacksonville. Weather-wise, it's a great start to the week — a high near 75 degrees, sunny skies, with a breezy northeast wind around 15 miles an hour. One flag if you're heading to the beach: there is a rip current statement in effect through early Tuesday morning, so use caution in the surf.

JENNY: The big local development story is the University of Florida's downtown Jacksonville campus taking a major step forward. UF has selected Edgemoor Infrastructure and Real Estate as the lead developer for the initial phase of its Jacksonville graduate campus — a first building valued at two hundred eighty-seven and a half million dollars. Edgemoor is bringing in a team that includes Clark Construction and local contractor Auld and White Constructors, so there's a Jacksonville presence in the build from the start.

ANDREW: This has been in the works for a while. What does the timeline look like?

JENNY: UF says it will open initial programs in existing downtown facilities this year, while new construction gets underway. The longer vision is a full research and graduate campus that the city sees as a talent anchor — the kind of institution that produces the workforce pipeline companies like Winn-Dixie are here to draw from. And speaking of Winn-Dixie: the twelve-million-dollar incentive package that cleared the Mayor's Budget Review Committee last week is set for formal introduction to City Council on April 28th. In exchange for the incentives — a combination of a recapture grant and a headquarters retention grant — Winn-Dixie commits to a sixty-five-million-dollar investment, retaining five hundred existing jobs and creating two hundred new positions at an average salary above a hundred thousand dollars. The MBRC vote was unanimous, and the April 28th introduction will be the first full Council review.

ANDREW: Any organized opposition at this point?

JENNY: Nothing public yet, but the Council introduction will surface scrutiny around the incentive-per-job math and the 2031 milestone structure. The deal is performance-based, which gives it defensibility, but these packages always draw questions in a public hearing. We will follow it.

JENNY: And the JEA situation continues to simmer. The four active investigations — State Attorney Melissa Nelson's subpoena, the City Council Special Investigatory Committee, and two others — are all running in parallel. No new indictments or subpoenas emerged this weekend. The situation between CEO Vickie Cavey, Council President Kevin Carrico, and the Ballard Partners lobbying connection remains unresolved. This one will be with us for a while.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week — and it's the most consequential single moment we have on the calendar. Wednesday, April 22nd is the expiration of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire. The specific thing to watch for is whether Iran's Foreign Ministry changes its current posture — which, as of Sunday, was a flat "no plan" for new talks — before Tuesday evening. If Tehran confirms attendance at the Pakistan talks before Wednesday, you will likely see an extension announced, oil will fall, and equities will rally. If Tuesday night comes and goes with no Iranian signal, you are looking at a probable breakdown at midnight Wednesday, with the risk of resumed strikes and an oil shock that reshapes the economic picture for the rest of the second quarter. Watch that Foreign Ministry statement. That is your leading indicator.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Monday. Have a great start to the week.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Lebanon Ceasefire Tested, Markets Hit Records, Builders Lose Confidence
item.description: A fragile Israel-Lebanon ceasefire takes effect amid reported violations as forty countries meet on reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Stocks surge to all-time highs. Homebuilder sentiment drops to a seven-month low.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Friday, April 17th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: A ten-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon has officially taken hold, but the Lebanese army is already reporting Israeli shelling in the south. And as we speak, about forty countries are meeting to figure out how to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

ANDREW: Plus, homebuilder confidence just hit its lowest point since last September. We'll break down what that signals for the spring housing market.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at a record 7,041, up about a quarter of a percent. The Dow added 115 points to finish at 48,579. And the Nasdaq settled at 24,103, up about a third of a percent, notching its twelfth consecutive daily gain, the longest winning streak since July 2009. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.27 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is at 6.30 percent according to Freddie Mac. The rally continues to be driven by optimism that the Iran conflict may be winding down.

ANDREW: And that optimism has a lot of moving pieces behind it, so let's unpack the Middle East first. As we've been tracking all week, the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports is now in its fifth day. The Pentagon says thirteen ships have been turned away.
